---
title: "Is Package Software Dead?"
format: html
---

## Introduction

AI has dramatically reduced the cost of building software—but not the cost of owning it.  

So why do enterprises still rely so heavily on package software?

---

## What is Package Software?

By package software, we mean off-the-shelf products deployed within an enterprise—either as traditional on-premise applications or SaaS platforms.

---

## Why Do Enterprises Use It?

### Lack of IT skills  
Many enterprises simply don’t have the capability to design, build and operate software at scale.

### Core competency  
If your business is centred around retail, banking or logistics, building and running a high-performing engineering organisation can be a significant cultural and operational shift. Many companies choose packages so they can stay focused on their core business.

### Lack of differentiation  
If a capability doesn’t provide competitive advantage, it rarely makes sense to build it yourself—using a standard package is often more efficient.

### Regulation  
In heavily regulated domains, compliance requirements can make custom development costly and risky.

### Complex IT estate  
In large enterprises with organically grown legacy systems, package software often provides mature integration capabilities that are difficult to replicate.

### Support, scalability and reliability  
Package software is typically proven at scale, with dedicated vendor support and architectures that have been hardened across many customers.

---

## Disadvantages

- Vendor lock-in  
- Limited differentiation—you get the same features as everyone else  
- High cost  
- Limited control—you depend on vendor roadmaps  

---

## What Has Changed?

AI has significantly reduced the cost and time required to build software, making custom solutions far more accessible than before.  

But does that invalidate the traditional advantages of package software? Let’s reassess each one.

---

In reality, we’re not there yet.  

The cost of building and, more importantly, operating software is still significant. For most enterprises, package solutions remain the safer and more economical choice today.  

However, this is the area to watch. As AI continues to reduce development and operational costs, the economics may shift—particularly if package vendors continue to push pricing upwards.

---

## Re-evaluating the Trade-offs

### Lack of IT skills  
AI reduces the need for large engineering teams, but it doesn’t remove the need for architecture, governance and operational ownership.

### Core competency  
While AI lowers the barrier to building software, it doesn’t change organisational culture. Many enterprises still struggle to operate as product-led organisations.

### Lack of differentiation  
If a capability offers no competitive advantage, packages remain the logical choice.  

However, AI reduces the cost of building bespoke solutions, making it increasingly viable to customise areas that were previously considered commodity.

### Regulation  
This remains a strong reason to use package software. AI does little to reduce the burden of compliance, auditability and legal accountability.

### Complex IT estate  
Package software still offers advantages in complex environments, although agentic AI may begin to reduce the need for tightly integrated monolithic systems by acting as a more flexible orchestration layer.

### Support, scalability and reliability  
AI improves monitoring and recovery, but operational responsibility still sits with the enterprise.

---

## The Real Advantage: Risk Transfer  

Beyond functionality, package software transfers a significant amount of risk away from the enterprise.  

- Security vulnerabilities  
- Regulatory compliance  
- System resilience and uptime  
- Data integrity  

When you build your own system, you own these risks entirely.  
With a package, that responsibility is shared with the vendor.  

This is one of the primary reasons package software remains so dominant—and something AI does little to change.

---

## Conclusion

Package software isn’t dead.  

But the economics are starting to shift.  

Smaller vendors without strong differentiation or regulatory moats will find it increasingly difficult to justify their pricing.  

Large enterprise platforms, deeply embedded in complex environments, are far more resilient—but even they are not immune to long-term pressure.  

You don’t need to rip them out today.  
But you do need to start questioning where you truly need them—and where you don’t.
